summary: O'Neil explains why she deleted her LinkedIn account before Microsoft's $26 billion acquisition, citing spam, bogus requests, and 'hyped up crap about magic bullets using big data' as reasons. She argues the Microsoft acquisition represents a significant drop in alignment between the platform and its users' interests. The post warns that years of professional social data people have carefully built will now belong to a corporation that can change privacy policies at will. She suspects Microsoft will use the network for marketing experiments and urges readers to delete their accounts.
key_points: LinkedIn had already become a spam-filled platform pushing big data hype before the acquisition
The $26 billion Microsoft acquisition transfers valuable personal professional data to a corporation with different incentives
Privacy policies can be changed unilaterally, leaving users with no real protection
Data that individuals protect as personally valuable becomes extremely valuable when aggregated by corporations
Corporate acquisitions represent a 'severe drop in alignment' between platform and user interests
money_quotes: The content I saw when I bothered to log in were consistently hyped up crap about magic bullets using big data.

All that social data, the professional contacts and so on that people have built up over years and years, is being handed over to a huge and powerful corporation that can change the privacy policy whenever they'd like.
This is data that people protect as personally valuable individually; it is clearly quite valuable more generally.
I also suspect they'll submit the LinkedIn network to experiments to see how professionals respond to various marketing campaigns. Yuck.
I'm not saying that LinkedIn had motives that were perfectly aligned with its users, but I'm seeing this as a severe drop in alignment.
key_insight: When platforms are acquired, years of carefully cultivated personal data get transferred to new corporate owners with different agendas and the power to change privacy rules unilaterally.
